title: Middle-Income Family: College Planning and Insurance Gaps
goals: Fund college for two children (ages 10 and 14)
Protect family income with adequate insurance
Reach retirement at 65 with sufficient savings
constraints: Competing demands: college savings vs. retirement
4 years to oldest child's college enrollment
key_risks: Roberto's income is primary; no own-occupation disability
Life insurance gap: $500K coverage vs $2.1M need
College funding gap: $14K saved vs $160-300K needed

planning_issues.description: Life insurance gap: $500K coverage vs. $2.1M need (10x income benchmark); own-occupation disability missing
$14,000 in 529 plans vs. $160,000–$300,000 college need for two children; 4-year runway for oldest
$142,000 at age 42 on $148K income — underfunded; target $296,000–$444,000 by this age
Tight surplus of $1,800/month after expenses; competing savings demands

key_recommendations: Increase life insurance to $1.5M (Roberto) + $500K (Maria) via term policies
Front-load 529 plans for older child ($15K/year, consider superfunding)
Increase 401k contributions to capture full match plus beyond
Evaluate financial aid strategy before over-saving in 529s
follow_up_questions: Does Roberto have employer-provided disability? Is it own-occupation definition?
What colleges are the children targeting — in-state public or private?
Has the family considered the 529 account ownership impact on financial aid?
Is Maria's part-time bookkeeping work growing toward full-time?
outcome_summary: Increased life insurance, added disability coverage, increased 529 contributions to $10,000/year per child. Financial aid FAFSA strategy established for oldest child.
key_lesson: Middle-income families must balance competing financial priorities. Insurance gaps are often the biggest risk; life and disability coverage should be addressed before accelerating discretionary goals.
